request.question: What genre of music that is highly dependent on technological developments does Jenn Vix record?

title: Techno-progressivism
Jenn Vix
History of ethanol fuel in Brazil
History of thermodynamics
Science and technology in Indonesia
DJ Vix
Electronics in rock music
Technological transitions
No innovation without representation
List of steampunk works
sentences: Techno-progressivism or tech-progressivism is a stance of active support for the convergence of technological change and social change.

Techno-progressives argue that technological developments can be profoundly empowering and emancipatory when they are regulated by legitimate democratic and accountable authorities to ensure that their costs, risks and benefits are all fairly shared by the actual stakeholders to those developments.

Jenn Vix is an American solo electronic-rock recording artist, songwriter, producer, and recording engineer, who has ventured into other genres including alternative rock, trip hop, darkwave, modern new wave and electro-industrial.

The history of ethanol fuel in Brazil dates from the 1970s and relates to Brazil's sugarcane-based ethanol fuel program, which allowed the country to become the world's second largest producer of ethanol, and the world's largest exporter.

Several important political and technological developments led Brazil to become the world leader in the sustainable use of bioethanol, and a policy model for other developing countries in the tropical zone of Latin America, the Caribbean, and Africa.

Government policies and technological advances also allowed the country to achieve a landmark in ethanol consumption, when ethanol retail sales surpassed 50% market share of the gasoline-powered vehicle fleet in early 2008.
This level of ethanol fuel consumption had only been reached in Brazil once before, at the peak of the "Pró-Álcool" Program near the end of the 1980s.
The history of thermodynamics is a fundamental strand in the history of physics, the history of chemistry, and the history of science in general.

Owing to the relevance of thermodynamics in much of science and technology, its history is finely woven with the developments of classical mechanics, quantum mechanics, magnetism, and chemical kinetics, to more distant applied fields such as meteorology, information theory, and biology (physiology), and to technological developments such as the steam engine, internal combustion engine, cryogenics and electricity generation.
The development of thermodynamics both drove and was driven by atomic theory.
It also, albeit in a subtle manner, motivated new directions in probability and statistics; see, for example, the timeline of thermodynamics.
Indonesia is not considered as one of the leading countries in science and technology developments.
However, there are many examples of notable scientific and technological developments and achievements contributed by Indonesians.
Despite being a developing country, Indonesia is one of a handful nations that have developed their own aerospace technology.
DJ Vix, born and raised in the east of London is a British-Asian DJ/music producer known for his bhangra tracks, as well as his remixes.
In 1996, he formed Dhol n Bass Roadshow that won many awards in coming years.
He has worked in many places across the globe including Vancouver, Rotterdam, Africa, Norway, Melbourne, Sydney, Perth, LA, Chicago, Washington DC, New York, Jumeirah and Delhi (Club Elevate).
Guest mixes and shows on the Asian radio scene have included for the likes of BBC Radio 1, BBC 1xtra and BBC Asian Network.
Signed up in 2002 by the internationally renowned Moviebox label, DJ Vix has received international recognition as a producer of mainstream Asian beats, with a style identical to none (album).

Following on from his debut album DHOL N BASS UNCUT (2002), he produced his next three albums VIX IT UP (2004) and IDENTICAL 2 NONE (2007) and MOVIEBOX VIX TAPE (2008) with 'Ah Chak Bottle Daru Di' featuring Shin from DCS - the longest no.1 track on the BBC Asian Network that held top spot for a record breaking 13 weeks.
In 2009 DJ Vix received his third award, 'Best Club DJ' at the UK Asian Music Awards (UK AMA) held at the Royal Festival Hall.
In 2011, Dj Vix worked in collaboration with Surinder Shinda and Bhinda Jatt (also known as California Bhangra King).
Dj Vix has also worked with artists like Miss Pooja, RnB superstar Jay Sean, Sukbir, Taz Stereo Nation, B21, XLNC, Debi Muksuspuri, H-Dhami and Ajay the ‘Brit Asia Superstar’ winner of 2010.
In 2014, Dj Vix worked in collaboration with Malkit Singh MBE, and produced the track'Desi Beat'.
In 2014, he released another track, 'Marda' feat Hunterz, along with the new album 'MY TURN' from Manjit Pappu.
His next, and fifth solo album titled "Chapter V" is scheduled to be released on 26 February 2015.
The use of electronic music technology in rock music coincided with the practical availability of electronic musical instruments and the genre's emergence as a distinct style.
Rock music has been highly dependent on technological developments, particularly the invention and refinement of the synthesizer, the development of the MIDI digital format and computer technology.
Technological innovations have occurred throughout history and rapidly increased over the modern age.
New technologies are developed and co-exist with the old before supplanting them.
Transport offers several examples; from sailing to steam ships to automobiles replacing horse-based transportation.
Technological transitions (TT) describe how these technological innovations occur and are incorporated into society.

Alongside the technological developments TT considers wider societal changes such as “user practices, regulation, industrial networks (supply, production, distribution), infrastructure, and symbolic meaning or culture”.
For a technology to have use, it must be linked to social structures human agency and organisations to fulfil a specific need.
Hughes refers to the ‘seamless web’ where physical artefacts, organisations, scientific communities, and social practices combine.

A technological system includes technical and non-technical aspects, and it a major shift in the socio-technical configurations (involving at least one new technology) is when a technological transition occurs.
No innovation without representation is a democratic ideal of ensuring that everyone involved gets a chance to be represented fairly in technological developments.

Langdon Winner (Thomas Phelan Chair of Humanities and Social Sciences in the Department of Science and Technology Studies at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute of Troy, NY) states that groups and social interests likely to be affected by a particular kind of technological change ought to be represented at an early stage in defining exactly what that technology will be.
It is the idea that relevant parties have a say in technological developments and are not left in the dark.
It has been spoken about by current professor Massimiano Bucchi (Ph.D.
Social and Political science, European University Institute, 1997) of the University of Trento in Italy.

This ideal does not require the public to become experts on the topics of science and engineering, it only asks that the opinions and ideas be heard before making drastic decisions, as talked about by Steven L. Goldman (Andrew W. Mellon Distinguished Professor in Humanities at Lehigh University).
Steampunk is a subgenre of fantasy and speculative fiction that came into prominence in the 1980s and early 1990s.

The term denotes works set in an era or world wherein steam power is still widely used—usually the 19th century, and often set in Victorian era England—but with prominent elements of either science fiction or fantasy, such as fictional technological inventions like those found in the works of H. G. Wells and Jules Verne, or real technological developments like the computer occurring at an earlier date.

Other examples of steampunk contain alternate history-style presentations of "the path not taken" of such technology as dirigibles or analog computers; these frequently are presented in an idealized light, or with a presumption of functionality.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What genre of music that is highly dependent on technological developments does Jenn Vix record?
hop1_docs: Jenn Vix | Jenn Vix is an American solo electronic-rock recording artist, songwriter, producer, and recording engineer, who has ventured into other genres including alternative rock, trip hop, darkwave, modern new wave and electro-industrial.

Electronics in rock music | The use of electronic music technology in rock music coincided with the practical availability of electronic musical instruments and the genre's emergence as a distinct style.  Rock music has been highly dependent on technological developments, particularly the invention and refinement of the synthesizer, the development of the MIDI digital format and computer technology.

Vix Technology | Vix Technology (Vix) is a global technology company that designs, supplies and operates mobility solutions for the public transit industry, including automated fare collection (AFC) systems, intelligent transportation systems (ITS) and passenger information display systems (PIDS).

List of electronic music genres | This is a list of electronic music genres, consisting of genres of electronic music, primarily created with electronic musical instruments or electronic music technology.  A distinction has been made between sound produced using electromechanical means and that produced using electronic technology.  Examples of electromechanical sound producing devices include the telharmonium, Hammond organ, and the electric guitar.  Purely electronic sound production can be achieved using devices such as the Theremin, sound synthesizer, and computer.  Genre however is not always dependent on instrumentation.

British pop music | British pop music is popular music, produced commercially in the United Kingdom.  It emerged in the mid-to late 1950s as a softer alternative to rock 'n' roll and later to rock music.  Like American pop music it has a focus on commercial recording, often orientated towards a youth market, as well as that of the Singles Chart usually through the medium of relatively short and simple love songs.  While these basic elements of the genre have remained fairly constant, pop music has absorbed influences from most other forms of popular music, particularly borrowing from the development of rock music, and utilising key technological innovations to produce new variations on existing themes.  From the British Invasion of the 1960s, led by The Beatles, British pop music has alternated between acts and genres with national appeal and those with international success that have had a considerable impact on the development of the wider genre and on popular music in general.

Jenne Lennon | Jenne Lennon is a Celtic singer.  In 2012, she received the Distinguished Alumni Award from the Elgin Children's Chorus.  Jenne has performed all over the United States and Europe headlining events such as the Festival Interceltique de Lorient in France, the Bergen Music Festival in Norway, and the Unity Temple in the United States.  She has worked and recorded with the likes of Emmy nominated composer Sarah Class, the Elgin Children's Chorus, Global Voices, the Academy of Irish Music and Baal Tinne.  Jenne is also a skilled Native American flute player and appeared in an episode of MTV' s Stand in performing with Sting.
What Does Good Luck Bring? | What Does Good Luck Bring?  is the second and final studio album by American indie rock band No. 2, released in 2002 by record label In Music We Trust.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Jenn Vix: passage [1] — "Jenn Vix is an American solo electronic-rock recording artist" (passage [1]).
  - Tech-dependence claim: passage [2] — "Rock music has been highly dependent on technological developments" (passage [2]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Candidate genre labels in the passages: "electronic-rock" (passage [1]); "rock music" / "Electronics in rock music" (passage [2]); broader "electronic music genres" (passage [4]).
  - Exact expected page "Electronic rock" is not present among the provided passages (only related pages listed).
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as written in passages): "electronic-rock" (from passage [1]).
  - Missing fact needed to answer the question: an explicit source statement linking "electronic-rock" (or Jenn Vix's recorded genre) to being "highly dependent on technological developments" — i.e., confirmation that the genre Jenn Vix records is the same genre described as tech-dependent.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open "Electronics in rock music" page — check the lede/first paragraph and any "Characteristics" or "History" sections for the explicit phrase that rock (or subgenres) are "highly dependent on technological developments" and whether "electronic rock" is mentioned as a subgenre.
     - Search keywords: "Electronics in rock music lede synthesizer MIDI 'highly dependent'".
  2) Open "Electronic rock" page (or disambiguation for "Electronic rock") — check lede/infobox and "Characteristics/History" for statements about reliance on technology.
     - Search keywords: "Electronic rock dependence on technology 'synthesizer' 'computer'".
  3) Open "List of electronic music genres" — check entry for "electronic rock" (if present) and any notes about instrumentation/technology dependence.
     - Search keywords: "List of electronic music genres electronic rock entry".
  4) Re-open or fetch full "Jenn Vix" page (lede/infobox) to confirm exact genre label(s) and citations supporting that label.
     - Search keywords: "Jenn Vix electronic-rock lede citations".

Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Electronics in rock music | The use of electronic music technology in rock music coincided with the practical availability of electronic musical instruments and the genre's emergence as a distinct style.  Rock music has been highly dependent on technological developments, particularly the invention and refinement of the synthesizer, the development of the MIDI digital format and computer technology.

List of electronic music genres | This is a list of electronic music genres, consisting of genres of electronic music, primarily created with electronic musical instruments or electronic music technology.  A distinction has been made between sound produced using electromechanical means and that produced using electronic technology.  Examples of electromechanical sound producing devices include the telharmonium, Hammond organ, and the electric guitar.  Purely electronic sound production can be achieved using devices such as the Theremin, sound synthesizer, and computer.  Genre however is not always dependent on instrumentation.

List of abbreviations in photography | During most of the 20th century photography depended mainly upon the photochemical technology of silver halide emulsions on glass plates or roll film.  Early in the 21st century this technology was displaced by the electronic technology of digital cameras.  The development of digital image sensors, microprocessors, memory cards, miniaturised devices and image editing software enabled these cameras to offer their users a much wider range of operating options than was possible with the older silver halide technology.  This has led to a proliferation of new abbreviations, acronyms and initialisms.  The commonest of these are listed below.  Some are used in related fields of optics and electronics but many are specific to digital photography.

Kreidler (band) | Kreidler is a German band from Düsseldorf, which was founded in 1994.  The band combines electronic and analog instruments, and is categorized by critics, depending on the publication, as electronic music, pop, avant-garde, post rock, IDM, ambient, neoclassical, krautrock, or electronica.

Science and technology in Asia | Science and technology in Asia is varied depending on the country and time.  In the past, the Asian civilizations most notable for their contributions to science and technology were India, China and the West Asian civilizations.  At present, probably the most notable country in Asia in terms of its technological and scientific achievement is Japan, which is particularly known for its electronics and automobile products.  In recent years, China and India have also once again become major contributors to science and technology.  Other countries are also notable in other scientific fields such as chemical and physics achievements.

Bill Viola | Bill Viola (born 1951) is a contemporary video artist whose artistic expression depends upon electronic, sound, and image technology in New Media.  His works focus on the ideas behind fundamental human experiences such as birth, death and aspects of consciousness.

Zooropa | Zooropa is the eighth studio album by Irish rock band U2.  Produced by Flood, Brian Eno, and The Edge, it was released on 5 July 1993 on Island Records.  Inspired by the band's experiences on the Zoo TV Tour, "Zooropa" expanded on many of the tour's themes of technology and media oversaturation.  The record was a continuation of the group's experimentation with alternative rock, electronic dance music, and electronic sound effects that began with their previous album, "Achtung Baby", in 1991.
response.summary_2: Jenn Vix is an American solo electronic-rock recording artist. Rock music has been highly dependent on technological developments, particularly the invention and refinement of the synthesizer, the development of the MIDI digital format and computer technology.
